prompt_md: Which of the following is the correct category for the `return` statement?

options.a: Conditional statement — it chooses between two paths
options.b: Loop — it repeats a block of code
options.c: Jump — it transfers execution unconditionally to another location
options.d: Function definition — it defines a new callable block

explanation_md: `return` transfers execution from the current function back to the caller without testing any condition, which makes it a jump. Conditionals test a condition first; loops repeat; function definitions describe callable units.
`x` is 3, so `x > 0` is true and `Positive` prints. The second `cout` is outside the `if` — it has no braces and is a separate statement — so it always runs, printing `Done`.

prompt_md: A program needs to print a table of square numbers for every integer from 1 to 20. Which control flow category is most appropriate?

options.a: Conditional statement — check whether each number is a square
options.b: Loop — repeat the same operation a fixed number of times
options.c: Jump — skip to the end when done
options.d: Function call — define a square function and call it once

explanation_md: When the same operation must be repeated a known (or variable) number of times, a loop is the right tool. Conditionals choose between paths; jumps exit constructs early; a function call alone would require the caller to invoke it 20 times explicitly.
`x` is -1, so `x > 0` is false. Execution skips the `then` branch and runs the `else` branch, printing `Not positive`.
